trial_name: DEVICE — Video vs Direct Laryngoscopy for Tracheal Intubation of Critically Ill Adults (2023)
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
citation: Prekker ME, Driver BE, Trent SA, et al. Video versus direct laryngoscopy for tracheal intubation of critically ill adults. N Engl J Med. 2023;389(5):418-429.
trial_type: RCT — multicenter, pragmatic, parallel-group, open-label, superiority
one_liner: In critically ill adults, a video-first laryngoscopy strategy increased first-attempt intubation success by 14.3 percentage points compared to direct laryngoscopy, with no significant difference in a composite of severe peri-intubation complications.

pico.population: Adults (≥18 years) in EDs and ICUs requiring emergency orotracheal intubation, where the operator believed either laryngoscopy approach was reasonable.
pico.intervention: Assignment to a video laryngoscope for the first intubation attempt.
pico.comparison: Assignment to a direct laryngoscope for the first intubation attempt.
pico.outcome: Successful intubation on the first attempt.

background.existing_knowledge: Emergency tracheal intubation in the ED/ICU frequently requires repeated attempts and is associated with peri-intubation hypoxaemia and cardiovascular instability. Video laryngoscopy improves glottic visualisation, but earlier ICU and ED trials showed mixed effects on first-pass success and complications.
background.knowledge_gap: Contemporary systematic reviews/meta-analyses in critically ill adults continued to show heterogeneity by setting, operator experience, and device type, with uncertainty about whether improved visualisation translates into fewer complications or better patient-centred outcomes.
background.hypothesis: In critically ill adults undergoing emergency tracheal intubation in the ED/ICU, initial use of a video laryngoscope (vs a direct laryngoscope) will increase successful intubation on the first attempt without increasing peri-intubation severe complications.

methods.design: Pragmatic, multicentre, parallel-group, randomised superiority trial conducted in 17 emergency departments and ICUs; open-label (no blinding) with concealed allocation using sealed opaque envelopes; outcome data collected by trained independent observers when feasible.

inclusion: Adults (≥18 years) located in a participating unit with a planned orotracheal intubation using a laryngoscope, performed by a clinician who routinely performs tracheal intubation.
The operator believed that either laryngoscopy approach could reasonably be used (i.e., no perceived requirement for a specific approach for that patient).
exclusion: An immediate need for intubation that precluded safe performance of the randomisation process
Operator believed one approach was necessary or that one approach was contraindicated

methods.intervention: Assignment to a video laryngoscope for the first intubation attempt (first attempt defined by a single laryngoscope blade insertion and either a single tracheal tube insertion or a bougie insertion followed by tracheal tube insertion); video laryngoscope type/blade geometry left to clinician discretion (pragmatic).
methods.comparison: Assignment to a direct laryngoscope for the first intubation attempt; subsequent attempts and adjuncts (e.g., bougie, stylet) at clinician discretion (pragmatic).
methods.blinding: Unblinded (operators and bedside team not blinded); the primary outcome is operationally defined and intended to be objectively observed; lack of blinding increases risk of performance bias (choice of adjuncts/backup strategy) and detection bias for more subjective complications.
methods.sample_size_power: Power calculation targeted a 5% absolute increase in first-attempt success (from 80% to 85%) with 90% power at a two-sided 5% significance level; planned enrolment 1920 patients (inflated to 2000 allowing for up to 4% missing data), with one interim analysis at 1000 patients and a Haybittle–Peto-style efficacy boundary of P≤0.001.
methods.primary_outcome: Successful intubation on the first attempt.
secondary_outcomes: Severe complication during intubation (composite: SpO2<80%, SBP <65 mmHg or new/increased vasopressor, or cardiac arrest from induction to 2 min after intubation)
Successful intubation on first laryngoscope blade insertion
Successful first attempt without severe complication
methods.follow_up: Peri-procedural outcomes measured during the intubation sequence (from induction through shortly after intubation); ICU-free days, ventilator-free days, and in-hospital death assessed up to 28 days after enrolment.

headline_bullets: Trial stopped early for efficacy after an interim analysis at 1420 enrolled patients.
Video laryngoscopy increased first-attempt success by 14.3 percentage points (85.1% vs 70.8%).
The prespecified severe-complication composite was similar between groups (21.4% vs 20.9%).
Exploratory analysis suggested a larger absolute benefit for operators with less experience.

outcomes_table.note: First attempt = 1 laryngoscope blade insertion and 1 tube insertion (or bougie + tube insertion).
Composite from induction to 2 min after intubation: SpO2<80%, SBP <65 mmHg or new/increased vasopressor, or cardiac arrest.
Duration from laryngoscope insertion to laryngoscope removal (or end of first attempt).

outcomes_table.outcome: Successful intubation on first laryngoscope blade insertion (exploratory)
Successful first attempt without severe complication (exploratory)
outcomes_table.note: Exploratory composite of procedural success and safety.

safety: Severe complication composite (SpO2<80%, SBP <65 mmHg or new/increased vasopressor, or cardiac arrest) was similar between groups: 21.4% vs 20.9% (absolute difference +0.5 pp; 95% CI −3.9 to 4.9).
Oesophageal intubation: 0.9% vs 1.3% (absolute difference −0.4 pp; 95% CI −1.5 to 0.7).
Aspiration: 1.0% vs 1.7% (absolute difference −0.7 pp; 95% CI −2.1 to 0.8).
In-hospital death within 1 hour: 2.1% vs 3.8% (absolute difference −1.7 pp; 95% CI −3.6 to 0.2).

domains.notes: Block randomisation stratified by site, implemented via sealed opaque envelopes; allocation revealed only immediately before laryngoscopy, supporting concealment and reducing selection bias.

Unblinded operators and teams could influence adjunct choices and escalation strategies; primary endpoint is largely procedural/objective, and observer-based capture was intended to reduce misclassification.

Three patients identified after enrolment as prisoners were excluded from subsequent data collection and analysis (pragmatic exclusion that introduces a small risk of bias if not random). Primary analysis was by intention-to-treat.

Laryngoscope on first attempt was video in 705/705 (100%) of the video group and direct in 704/712 (98.9%) of the direct group (video crossover 8/712, 1.1%). Randomisation applied only to the first attempt; subsequent attempts were at clinician discretion.
Key baseline variables were well balanced (e.g., age median 55 vs 55 years; APACHE II median 16 vs 16; ED location 69.7% vs 70.2%).

internal_validity.verdict: Overall, internal validity appears strong for the primary procedural endpoint (concealed randomisation, minimal crossover, explicit endpoint definitions), with moderate limitations for safety/clinical outcomes due to open-label conduct, some missing physiological data, and early stopping.

domains.notes: ED/ICU adults undergoing emergency intubation reflect a common real-world critically ill airway population; however, very time-critical cases (where randomisation could not be safely performed), pregnancy, and prisoners were excluded, and clinicians could exclude patients when they believed one approach was required or contraindicated.

Most intubations were performed by emergency medicine residents or critical care fellows (91.5%), which is typical of many academic ED/ICU systems but may differ from settings where anaesthetists are the primary operators. Benefit may depend on access to modern video laryngoscopes, training, and routine use of adjuncts (bougie/stylet) and physiological optimisation; translation to resource-limited settings or systems without reliable video equipment/training may be constrained. Findings directly apply to ED/ICU (not operating theatre, elective intubation, prehospital, or paediatric practice).

external_validity.verdict: Generalisability is moderate to high for adult ED/ICU airway management in similarly resourced systems, but is limited for very time-critical airways, settings lacking video equipment/training, and non-ED/ICU contexts.

strengths: High adherence to assigned first-attempt device
Clinically meaningful, operationally defined primary endpoint
Broad operator and setting mix (ED and ICU)
Detailed procedural and safety outcome capture
limitations: Unblinded trial with potential performance/detection bias for some outcomes
Early stopping for efficacy (risk of overestimating effect size for the primary endpoint)
Severe-complication composite may be underpowered for modest differences and relies on available physiological measurements
Device type/blade geometry and adjunct strategies were not standardised (pragmatic heterogeneity)

critical_appraisal.summary: A well-conducted, pragmatic trial with strong internal validity for its primary procedural endpoint, though open-label design and early stopping warrant caution in interpreting safety and clinical outcomes.
strengths: Concealed randomisation and high protocol adherence
Large sample size across multiple centres
weaknesses: Unblinded design introduces potential for bias in adjunct use and safety reporting
Early stopping may inflate the observed treatment effect
Safety composite underpowered and dependent on complete physiological data capture

sections.authors_conclusion: In critically ill adults undergoing tracheal intubation, the use of a video laryngoscope resulted in a higher incidence of successful intubation on the first attempt than the use of a direct laryngoscope.

clinical_bottom_line.verdict: A 'video-first' strategy should be the default approach for emergency tracheal intubation in the ED and ICU to maximise first-pass success.
clinical_bottom_line.applies_to: Critically ill adults in EDs and ICUs requiring intubation, particularly when performed by less experienced operators.
clinical_bottom_line.does_not_mean: Video laryngoscopy eliminates peri-intubation complications or improves mortality; it is one component of a comprehensive airway safety bundle.
implementation_caveats: Requires structured training in video laryngoscopy and maintenance of direct laryngoscopy skills for rescue.
Must be integrated with clear rescue pathways and physiological optimisation bundles.
Benefit may be greatest for operators with less experience.

controversies.notes: The earlier ICU multicentre MACMAN RCT reported similar first-pass success (67.7% video vs 70.3% direct) but higher severe life-threatening complications with video laryngoscopy (45.2% vs 27.1%); DEVICE’s contrasting findings highlight how operator experience, device/blade selection, adjunct use (bougie/stylet), and local airway workflows can modify real-world effectiveness.

Stopping after interim efficacy increases the probability that the observed primary effect size is overestimated; this is particularly relevant for technology adoption decisions where training and workflow change can alter baseline performance.

First-attempt success is a plausible safety surrogate but is not a patient-centred endpoint; DEVICE did not demonstrate clear differences in ICU-free days, ventilator-free days, or 28-day in-hospital mortality (all post hoc or exploratory), leaving uncertainty about downstream clinical benefit.

The severe-complication composite mixes hypoxaemia, haemodynamic collapse, and cardiac arrest; it is clinically coherent but sensitive to missing vital sign data and to the chosen observation window (induction to 2 minutes post-intubation).

context_related_trials.relation: An earlier ICU multicentre RCT that reported similar first-pass success but higher severe complications with video laryngoscopy, contrasting with DEVICE's findings and highlighting the impact of operator experience and local workflows.

unresolved_questions: Does a 'video-first' strategy improve patient-centred outcomes like mortality or ICU-free days?
What is the optimal device, blade geometry, and adjunct strategy for video laryngoscopy in critically ill adults?
How should training programs balance video and direct laryngoscopy skills to ensure competence in both?

numbers_to_remember: Severe complication composite: 21.4% vs 20.9% (no significant difference)
quick_recall.one_line_takeaway: Use a video laryngoscope as the default first device for emergency intubations in the ED and ICU to significantly improve first-pass success, but don't expect it to single-handedly reduce complications or mortality.
